---
title: "Scaling & encoding (a preview)"
module: "M3 — Data & Tooling"
chapter: 10
difficulty: "intermediate"
prereqs:
  - "M3 Ch4 — Cleaning data"
learning_objectives:
  - "Explain why features are scaled to a common range before modelling"
  - "Apply min-max scaling to [0,1] and standardization (z-score) to mean 0 / std 1"
  - "One-hot encode a category with pd.get_dummies so a model can read it as numbers"
compute: "browser"
status: "stable"
last_reviewed: "2026-06-29"
est_minutes: 18
resources:
  - ../../lib/grader.py
  - ../../lib/data.py
  - data/penguins.csv
pyodide:
  packages:
    - pandas
review_cards:
  - id: why-scale
    q: "Why do we scale features to a common range before modelling?"
    a: "Many models judge columns by their raw size, so a feature measured in thousands can drown out one measured in tenths even when both matter. Scaling puts every feature on comparable footing so each gets a fair say."
  - id: minmax-vs-zscore
    q: "What is the difference between min-max scaling and standardization?"
    a: "Min-max scaling squeezes a column into a fixed [0,1] range using its min and max. Standardization (z-score) subtracts the mean and divides by the standard deviation, giving mean 0 and std 1 with no fixed bounds — it is less disturbed by outliers' position."
  - id: one-hot
    q: "What does one-hot encoding do, and why is it needed?"
    a: "It turns one categorical column into several 0/1 columns, one per category, with a 1 in the column for that row's category. Models do arithmetic on numbers, not text labels, so a category has to become numeric — and one-hot avoids implying a false order between categories."
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light:
        - cosmo
        - ../../theme/larnix.scss
      dark:
        - darkly
        - ../../theme/larnix-dark.scss
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    include-after-body: ../../theme/_after-body.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---

{{< badge difficulty=intermediate >}} {{< badge compute=browser >}} {{< badge status=stable >}}

## The hook

Imagine judging a baking contest where one score is the cake's weight in grams
(say 1,200) and another is its sweetness on a scale of 0 to 10 (say 7). If you
naively add the scores, the weight swamps the sweetness — not because weight
matters more, but because its **numbers are bigger**. Many machine-learning models
fall into exactly this trap: they read a column measured in thousands as more
important than a column measured in tenths, purely because of its raw size.

This chapter is a **preview** of *feature preparation* — the step between a clean
table and a model. We won't train anything yet; the goal is to see the two moves
you'll reach for again and again:

- **Scaling** numeric columns onto a common range, so no feature wins on size alone.
- **Encoding** a text category into numbers, so a model can read it at all.

We'll use the Palmer Penguins again — a familiar table from earlier chapters — and
prepare two of its columns the way you would before handing them to a model.

> You'll need from before: loading a CSV and selecting columns (M3 Ch2), and
> cleaning out missing values (M3 Ch4). We'll assume the columns we touch are clean.

---

## Why models care about scale

A model that measures distance or sums up weighted features treats a column's raw
numbers at face value. Penguin **body mass** runs from about 2,700 to 6,300 grams;
**bill length** runs from about 32 to 60 millimetres. To such a model, a 100-gram
difference in mass looks a hundred times larger than a 1-millimetre difference in
bill length — even though, biologically, both might be equally telling.

Left unscaled, the big-numbered column dominates: it dictates which penguins look
"close" and which look "far apart", and the smaller column barely registers.
**Scaling** fixes this by rewriting every numeric column onto a shared range, so
each one contributes on its own merits rather than on the accident of its units.

Let's see the size gap first-hand, then close it.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Two columns on wildly different scales — mass in thousands, bill in tens"
import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins").dropna(subset=["body_mass_g", "bill_length_mm"])
print("body_mass_g  range:", df["body_mass_g"].min(), "to", df["body_mass_g"].max())
print("bill_length_mm range:", df["bill_length_mm"].min(), "to", df["bill_length_mm"].max())
```

One column lives in the thousands, the other in the tens. The two scaling recipes
below put them on equal footing — one by squeezing into a fixed window, the other
by re-centring around the average.

## Min-max scaling to [0, 1]

The simplest recipe is **min-max scaling**: slide and squeeze a column so its
smallest value becomes `0`, its largest becomes `1`, and everything else lands
proportionally in between. The formula is:

$$
x_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{x - \min}{\max - \min}
$$

Subtracting the minimum moves the smallest value to `0`; dividing by the *range*
(`max - min`) shrinks the largest down to `1`. Every column treated this way ends
up in the same `[0, 1]` window, whatever its original units.

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Min-max scaling body_mass_g into the [0,1] range"
import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins").dropna(subset=["body_mass_g"])
mass = df["body_mass_g"]

scaled = (mass - mass.min()) / (mass.max() - mass.min())
print("scaled min:", round(float(scaled.min()), 2))
print("scaled max:", round(float(scaled.max()), 2))
print("first three scaled:", [round(float(v), 3) for v in scaled.head(3)])
```

The scaled column now runs from exactly `0.0` to `1.0`. A penguin sitting halfway
between the lightest and heaviest birds scores around `0.5`. Because the whole
operation is vectorized, pandas applies it to all 342 values at once — no loop.

Min-max scaling is intuitive and keeps the *shape* of the data, but it has one
soft spot: a single very large outlier stretches the `max`, squashing every other
value into a thin band near zero. When outliers are a worry, the next recipe is
steadier.

## Standardization (z-score) to mean 0, std 1

**Standardization** re-expresses each value as *how many standard deviations it
sits from the mean*. That number is called a **z-score**:

$$
z = \frac{x - \text{mean}}{\text{std}}
$$

Subtracting the mean re-centres the column on `0`; dividing by the standard
deviation rescales the spread so the column's std becomes `1`. Unlike min-max,
there is no fixed ceiling — a z-score can be `-2.5` or `+3.1` — but typical values
cluster between about `-3` and `+3`. A z-score of `0` means "exactly average";
`+1` means "one standard deviation above average".

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "Standardizing body_mass_g to mean 0, std 1 (population std, ddof=0)"
import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins").dropna(subset=["body_mass_g"])
mass = df["body_mass_g"]

z = (mass - mass.mean()) / mass.std(ddof=0)
print("z mean:", round(float(z.mean()), 2))
print("z std:", round(float(z.std(ddof=0)), 2))
print("first three z-scores:", [round(float(v), 2) for v in z.head(3)])
```

The new column has mean `0.0` and std `1.0`, exactly as promised. Note the
`ddof=0`: that asks pandas for the **population** standard deviation (divide by
`n`), which is what most scaling tools — including scikit-learn's `StandardScaler`
— use. Pandas defaults to the *sample* std (divide by `n - 1`), so passing `ddof=0`
keeps our numbers aligned with the standard tool you'll meet later.

So which do you pick? Min-max when you want a guaranteed bounded range (some models
and image pipelines expect `[0, 1]`); standardization when your data has outliers
or roughly bell-shaped spread. Both share the same purpose: put every numeric
feature on comparable footing so the model weighs them by signal, not by size.

## One-hot encoding a category

Scaling handles *numbers*. But penguins also have a **species** column holding
text — `Adelie`, `Chinstrap`, `Gentoo`. A model that multiplies and adds cannot
read the word "Gentoo"; it needs numbers.

A tempting shortcut is to map the species to `0, 1, 2`. That quietly lies to the
model: it implies `Gentoo (2)` is "twice" `Chinstrap (1)` and that the categories
have an order, which they do not. **One-hot encoding** avoids the lie. It replaces
the single text column with one `0/1` column *per category*: a row gets a `1` in
the column for its own species and `0` everywhere else. No category is treated as
bigger than another.

Pandas does this in one call with `pd.get_dummies`:

```{pyodide}
#| caption: "One-hot encoding species into one 0/1 column per category"
import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins")
encoded = pd.get_dummies(df["species"])
print("new columns:", list(encoded.columns))
print("shape:", encoded.shape)
encoded.head()
```

One text column became **three** numeric columns — `Adelie`, `Chinstrap`,
`Gentoo` — one per species in the data. Read any row and exactly one of the three
holds a `1` (or `True`, pandas' 0/1 flavour): that row's species. The information
is identical to the original label, but now it is in a form a model can do
arithmetic on, with no false ordering implied.

In a full pipeline you would scale the numeric columns *and* one-hot the
categorical ones, then glue them back into a single table of numbers — the input a
model actually trains on. That assembly is a later chapter; for now you've met the
two moves it is built from.

---

## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaways}
### Key Takeaways

1. Many models read a column's **raw size** as importance, so features are
   **scaled** to a common range to give each a fair say.
2. **Min-max** scaling maps a column to `[0, 1]` with `(x - min) / (max - min)` —
   bounded, but sensitive to outliers.
3. **Standardization** (z-score) maps a column to mean `0`, std `1` with
   `(x - mean) / std` — unbounded, steadier with outliers; use `ddof=0` to match
   standard tools.
4. **One-hot encoding** with `pd.get_dummies` turns one text category into one
   `0/1` column per value, so a model reads it as numbers with no false order.
:::

---

## Practice

Each exercise is auto-graded by `run_tests`, loaded for you from `lib/grader.py`.
The first two use tiny hand-made lists so the answers are exact.

### Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢

Write `min_max_scale(values)`, which scales a list of numbers into `[0, 1]` using
the min-max formula. Replace `____`, then press **Run**.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_min_max
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_min_max
def min_max_scale(values):
    """Scale a list of numbers into the [0, 1] range with min-max scaling."""
    lo = min(values)
    hi = max(values)
    return ____  # TODO: [(v - lo) / (hi - lo) for v in values]

run_tests([
    ("0..10", min_max_scale([0, 5, 10]), [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]),
    ("shifted", min_max_scale([2, 4, 6]), [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

The lowest value should map to `0` and the highest to `1`. For each value `v`,
subtract the low end and divide by the range: `(v - lo) / (hi - lo)`. Build the
new list with a comprehension over `values`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
def min_max_scale(values):
    lo = min(values)
    hi = max(values)
    return [(v - lo) / (hi - lo) for v in values]

run_tests([
    ("0..10", min_max_scale([0, 5, 10]), [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]),
    ("shifted", min_max_scale([2, 4, 6]), [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]),
])
```

Subtracting `lo` moves the smallest value to `0`; dividing by `hi - lo` (the range)
pulls the largest down to `1`. The middle value of `[2, 4, 6]` lands at `0.5`,
exactly halfway.
</details>

### Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡

Write `standardize(values)`, which returns the z-score of each value, rounded to
two decimals. Use NumPy's **population** standard deviation (`np.array(...).std()`,
which divides by `n`). Complete the marked lines.

```{pyodide}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_standardize
from lib.grader import run_tests
```

```{pyodide}
#| exercise: ex_standardize
import numpy as np

def standardize(values):
    """Return each value's z-score, rounded to 2 decimals (population std)."""
    a = np.array(values, dtype=float)
    return ____  # TODO: [round(float((v - a.mean()) / a.std()), 2) for v in values]

run_tests([
    ("four values", standardize([2, 4, 6, 8]), [-1.34, -0.45, 0.45, 1.34]),
])
```

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`a.mean()` is the average and `a.std()` is the population standard deviation (NumPy
divides by `n` by default). For each value, compute `(v - a.mean()) / a.std()`,
wrap it in `float(...)`, and `round(..., 2)`. For `[2, 4, 6, 8]` the mean is `5` and
the std is `√5 ≈ 2.236`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show solution</summary>

```python
import numpy as np

def standardize(values):
    a = np.array(values, dtype=float)
    return [round(float((v - a.mean()) / a.std()), 2) for v in values]

run_tests([
    ("four values", standardize([2, 4, 6, 8]), [-1.34, -0.45, 0.45, 1.34]),
])
```

The mean of `[2, 4, 6, 8]` is `5` and the population std is `√5 ≈ 2.236`. So
`(2 - 5) / 2.236 ≈ -1.34` and `(8 - 5) / 2.236 ≈ 1.34`, with the two middle values
at `±0.45`. The z-scores are symmetric because the values are evenly spaced around
their mean.
</details>

### Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴

Open-ended, **rubric-graded** (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset and
one-hot encode the `species` column with `pd.get_dummies`. Report **how many new
columns** appear and what each one represents, then explain in a sentence why a
model needs numbers rather than the original text labels.

**Rubric — a good answer:**

- Loads penguins with `load_csv` and calls `pd.get_dummies` on `df["species"]`.
- Reports that **3** new columns appear (`Adelie`, `Chinstrap`, `Gentoo`) — one per
  species — and that each row has a single `1`/`True` marking its species.
- Explains that a model does arithmetic on numbers, so text categories must become
  numeric, and one-hot avoids implying a false order between the species.

<details><summary>If you're stuck</summary>

`pd.get_dummies(df["species"])` returns the encoded table. Its number of columns is
`encoded.shape[1]`, and `list(encoded.columns)` names them. Compare a row of the
encoded table to its original species to see the single `1`.
</details>

<details><summary>Show one possible answer</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins")
encoded = pd.get_dummies(df["species"])

print("new column count:", encoded.shape[1])
print("new columns:", list(encoded.columns))
print(encoded.head())
print(
    "A model multiplies and adds numbers, so 'Gentoo' must become numeric. "
    "One-hot uses one 0/1 column per species, so no species is treated as "
    "larger than another."
)
```

This prints `3` new columns — `Adelie`, `Chinstrap`, `Gentoo` — one per species,
each row carrying a single `True`. Mapping species to `0, 1, 2` instead would
falsely imply an order and relative size; one-hot encoding sidesteps that.
</details>

---

## Check yourself

{{< quiz quiz-ch10.yml >}}

---

## Where this fits / what's next

You've previewed the two everyday moves of feature preparation: **scaling** numeric
columns (min-max or z-score) so none wins on size alone, and **one-hot encoding** a
category so a model can read it as numbers. These are the bridge between a clean
table and a model's input. The actual modelling — fitting a line or a classifier to
prepared features and measuring how well it does — is where the next module picks
up.
